infobox.key: University of Notre Dame du Lac
infobox.value: (known simply as **Notre Dame**; [/ˌnoʊtərˈdeɪm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-University_of_Notre_Dame.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-University of Notre Dame.wav") [*NOH-tər-DAYM*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); **ND**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") Catholic [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Notre Dame, Indiana](/wiki/Notre_Dame,_Indiana "Notre Dame, Indiana"), United States.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The university was founded in 1842 by members of the [Congregation of Holy Cross](/wiki/Congregation_of_Holy_Cross "Congregation of Holy Cross"), a Catholic religious order of priests and brothers. Its [main campus](/wiki/Campus_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame "Campus of the University of Notre Dame") covers 1,261 acres (510 [ha](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare")) in a suburban setting and has landmarks such as the [Golden Dome](/wiki/Main_Building_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Main Building (University of Notre Dame)") main building, [Sacred Heart Basilica](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart_(Notre_Dame) "Basilica of the Sacred Heart (Notre Dame)"), the [Grotto of Our Lady of Lourdes](/wiki/Grotto_of_Our_Lady_of_Lourdes,_Notre_Dame "Grotto of Our Lady of Lourdes, Notre Dame"), the [Word of Life](/wiki/Word_of_Life_(mural) "Word of Life (mural)") mosaic mural, and [Notre Dame Stadium](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Stadium "Notre Dame Stadium").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Catholic university in Notre Dame, Indiana, U.S.

For other universities and colleges named "Notre Dame", see [Notre Dame (disambiguation)](/wiki/Notre_Dame_(disambiguation)#Colleges_and_universities "Notre Dame (disambiguation)").

The **University of Notre Dame du Lac** (known simply as **Notre Dame**; [/ˌnoʊtərˈdeɪm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-University_of_Notre_Dame.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-University of Notre Dame.wav") [*NOH-tər-DAYM*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); **ND**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") Catholic [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Notre Dame, Indiana](/wiki/Notre_Dame,_Indiana "Notre Dame, Indiana"), United States.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The university was founded in 1842 by members of the [Congregation of Holy Cross](/wiki/Congregation_of_Holy_Cross "Congregation of Holy Cross"), a Catholic religious order of priests and brothers. Its [main campus](/wiki/Campus_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame "Campus of the University of Notre Dame") covers 1,261 acres (510 [ha](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare")) in a suburban setting and has landmarks such as the [Golden Dome](/wiki/Main_Building_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Main Building (University of Notre Dame)") main building, [Sacred Heart Basilica](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart_(Notre_Dame) "Basilica of the Sacred Heart (Notre Dame)"), the [Grotto of Our Lady of Lourdes](/wiki/Grotto_of_Our_Lady_of_Lourdes,_Notre_Dame "Grotto of Our Lady of Lourdes, Notre Dame"), the [Word of Life](/wiki/Word_of_Life_(mural) "Word of Life (mural)") mosaic mural, and [Notre Dame Stadium](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Stadium "Notre Dame Stadium").

Notre Dame is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research spending and doctorate production".[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The university is organized into seven schools and colleges: [College of Arts and Letters](/wiki/Notre_Dame_College_of_Arts_and_Letters "Notre Dame College of Arts and Letters"), [College of Science](/wiki/Notre_Dame_College_of_Science "Notre Dame College of Science"), [Notre Dame Law School](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Law_School "Notre Dame Law School"), [School of Architecture](/wiki/Notre_Dame_School_of_Architecture "Notre Dame School of Architecture"), [College of Engineering](/wiki/Notre_Dame_College_of_Engineering "Notre Dame College of Engineering"), [Mendoza College of Business](/wiki/Mendoza_College_of_Business "Mendoza College of Business"), and [Keough School of Global Affairs](/wiki/Keough_School_of_Global_Affairs "Keough School of Global Affairs"). Notre Dame's graduate program includes more than 50 master, doctoral and professional degrees offered by the seven schools.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The university's athletic teams are members of the [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") and are known collectively as the [Fighting Irish](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish "Notre Dame Fighting Irish"). Notre Dame is noted for its [football team](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football"), which contributed to its rise to prominence on the national stage in the early 20th century.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Notre Dame teams in other sports, chiefly in the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference"), have won 17 national championships.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Between 1952 and 1987, [Theodore Hesburgh](/wiki/Theodore_Hesburgh "Theodore Hesburgh")'s administration increased the university's resources and improved its academic programs and its reputation. Its network of alumni consists of 151,000 members.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the University of Notre Dame](/wiki/History_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame "History of the University of Notre Dame")

### Foundations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundations")]

[![A bearded Sorin sits in a chair at a desk looking at the camera](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Rev_Edward_Sorin_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Rev_Edward_Sorin_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rev_Edward_Sorin_(cropped).jpg)

[Edward Sorin](/wiki/Edward_Sorin "Edward Sorin"), founder of the university, photographed in 1890

[Stephen Badin](/wiki/Stephen_Badin "Stephen Badin"), the first priest ordained in the United States, had come to northern Indiana after an invitation extended by [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi") chief [Leopold Pokagon](/wiki/Leopold_Pokagon "Leopold Pokagon") to minister to his tribe, and Badin bought the 524 acres (212 ha) of land in 1830 that became the university's campus. In 1842, bishop [Célestin Guynemer de la Hailandière](/wiki/C%C3%A9lestin_Guynemer_de_la_Hailandi%C3%A8re "Célestin Guynemer de la Hailandière") of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Vincennes](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Vincennes "Roman Catholic Diocese of Vincennes") offered the land to [Edward Sorin](/wiki/Edward_Sorin "Edward Sorin") of the [Congregation of Holy Cross](/wiki/Congregation_of_Holy_Cross "Congregation of Holy Cross") on the condition that he build a college in two years.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Sorin arrived on the site with eight Holy Cross brothers from France and Ireland on November 26, 1842 and began the school using Badin's old [log chapel](/wiki/Log_Chapel,_University_of_Notre_Dame "Log Chapel, University of Notre Dame"). After enrolling two students, Sorin erected more buildings, including the [Old College](/wiki/Old_College,_University_of_Notre_Dame "Old College, University of Notre Dame"), the first church, and the first main building.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Notre Dame began as a primary and secondary school; in 1844 it received its official college charter from the [Indiana General Assembly](/wiki/Indiana_General_Assembly "Indiana General Assembly"),[[18]](#cite_note-Hope,_C.S.C._1979-18) under the name of the University of Notre Dame du Lac (University of Our Lady of the Lake).[[a]](#cite_note-20) The university was originally all-male; the [Sisters of the Holy Cross](/wiki/Sisters_of_the_Holy_Cross "Sisters of the Holy Cross") founded the female-only [Saint Mary's College](/wiki/Saint_Mary%27s_College_(Indiana) "Saint Mary's College (Indiana)") near Notre Dame in 1844.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

The college awarded its first degrees in 1849.[[21]](#cite_note-22) As it grew under the presidency of Sorin and his successors, new academic programs were offered and new buildings built to accommodate the growing student and faculty population.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[18]](#cite_note-Hope,_C.S.C._1979-18) The brief presidency of Patrick Dillon (1865–1866) saw the original main building replaced with a larger one that housed the university's administration, classrooms, and dormitories. Under [William Corby](/wiki/William_Corby "William Corby")'s first administration, enrollment at Notre Dame increased to over 500 students. In 1869, he opened the [law school](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Law_School "Notre Dame Law School"), which offered a two-year course of study, and in 1871 he began construction of Sacred Heart Church, today the [Basilica of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart_(Notre_Dame) "Basilica of the Sacred Heart (Notre Dame)"). Two years later, the president [Auguste Lemonnier](/wiki/Auguste_Lemonnier "Auguste Lemonnier") started a library in the Main Building that had 10,000 volumes by 1879.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

[![The current Main Building with a golden dome](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Main_Building_at_the_University_of_Notre_Dame.jpg/250px-Main_Building_at_the_University_of_Notre_Dame.jpg)](/wiki/File:Main_Building_at_the_University_of_Notre_Dame.jpg)

The current [Main Building](/wiki/Main_Building_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Main Building (University of Notre Dame)"), built after the great fire of 1879

However, fire destroyed the Main Building and the library collection in April 1879; the school closed immediately and students were sent home.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Rebuilding began on May 17, and [the third and current Main Building](/wiki/Main_Building_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Main Building (University of Notre Dame)") was completed before the fall semester of 1879. The library collection was likewise redeveloped.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

The presidency of [Thomas E. Walsh](/wiki/Thomas_E._Walsh "Thomas E. Walsh") (1881–1893) focused on improving Notre Dame's scholastic reputation and standards. At the time, many students came to Notre Dame only for its business courses and did not graduate.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Walsh started a "[Belles Lettres](/wiki/Belles-lettres "Belles-lettres")" program and invited notable lay intellectuals like writer [Maurice Francis Egan](/wiki/Maurice_Francis_Egan "Maurice Francis Egan") to campus.[[27]](#cite_note-28) [Washington Hall](/wiki/Washington_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Washington Hall (University of Notre Dame)") was built in 1881 as a theater,[[28]](#cite_note-29) and the Science Hall (today the [LaFortune Student Center](/wiki/LaFortune_Student_Center "LaFortune Student Center")) was built in 1883 to house the science program (established in 1880) and multiple classrooms and science labs.[[29]](#cite_note-30) The construction of [Sorin Hall](/wiki/Sorin_Hall "Sorin Hall") saw the first freestanding residence hall on campus and one of the first in the country to have private rooms for students, a project championed by Sorin and John Zahm.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) During Walsh's tenure, Notre Dame started its [football program](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football") and awarded its first [Laetare Medal](/wiki/Laetare_Medal "Laetare Medal"), one the earlist such honors bequesthed by a Catholic university in the United States.[[32]](#cite_note-33) The Law School was reorganized under the leadership of [William J. Hoynes](/wiki/William_J._Hoynes "William J. Hoynes") (dean from 1883 to 1919), and when its new building was opened shortly after his death, it was renamed in his honor.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Connell2001-34)

### Growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Growth")]

[John Zahm](/wiki/John_Augustine_Zahm "John Augustine Zahm") was the Holy Cross Provincial for the United States from 1898 to 1906, with overall supervision of the university. He sought to modernize and expand Notre Dame by erecting buildings and adding to the campus art gallery and library, amassing what became a famous [Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") collection, and pushing Notre Dame toward becoming a research university dedicated to scholarship. The congregation did not renew Zahm's term, fearing he had expanded Notre Dame too quickly and had run the order into serious debt.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Connell2001-34) In particular, his vision to make Notre Dame a research university was at odds with that of [Andrew Morrissey](/wiki/Andrew_Morrissey "Andrew Morrissey"), president from 1893 to 1905, who had hoped to keep the institution a smaller boarding school.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Morrissey's presidency remained largely focused on younger students and saw the construction of the [Grotto](/wiki/Grotto_of_Our_Lady_of_Lourdes,_Notre_Dame "Grotto of Our Lady of Lourdes, Notre Dame"), the addition of wings to [Sorin Hall](/wiki/Sorin_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Sorin Hall (University of Notre Dame)"), and the erection of the first gymnasium. By 1900, student enrollment had increased to over 700, with most students still following the Commercial Course.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart%2C_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG/250px-Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart%2C_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG)](/wiki/File:Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart,_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG)

The [Basilica of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart_(Notre_Dame) "Basilica of the Sacred Heart (Notre Dame)"), completed in 1888

The movement toward a research university was championed subsequently by [John W. Cavanaugh](/wiki/John_W._Cavanaugh "John W. Cavanaugh"), who modernized educational standards and dedicated himself to the school's academic reputation and to increasing the number of students awarded bachelor's and master's degrees. As part of his efforts, he attracted many eminent scholars, established a chair in journalism, and introduced courses in [chemical engineering](/wiki/Chemical_engineering "Chemical engineering"). During his time as president, Notre Dame rapidly became a significant force on the football field.[[36]](#cite_note-37) In 1917, Notre Dame awarded its first degree to a woman and its first bachelor's degree in 1922. However, female undergraduates were uncommon until 1972.[[37]](#cite_note-38) [James A. Burns](/wiki/James_A._Burns "James A. Burns") became president in 1919 and following in the footsteps of Cavanaugh, he oversaw an academic revolution that brought the school up to national standards by adopting the elective system and moving away from the traditional scholastic and classical emphasis in three years.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) Notre Dame continued to grow, adding more colleges, programs, residence halls, and sports teams.[[40]](#cite_note-Archives_Chapter_4-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42) By 1921, with the addition of the [College of Commerce](/wiki/Mendoza_College_of_Business "Mendoza College of Business"),[[40]](#cite_note-Archives_Chapter_4-41) Notre Dame had grown from a small college to a university with five colleges and a law school.[[42]](#cite_note-Law2-43)

[![Drawing of the University of Notre Dame](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Catalogue_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame_%281903%29_%2814782351722%29.jpg/330px-Catalogue_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame_%281903%29_%2814782351722%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catalogue_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame_(1903)_(14782351722).jpg)

The University of Notre Dame in 1903

President [Matthew Walsh](/wiki/Matthew_J._Walsh "Matthew J. Walsh") (1922–1928) addressed the material needs of the university, particularly the $10,000 debt and the lack of space for new students. When he assumed the presidency, more than 1,100 students lived off campus while only 135 students paid for room and board. With fund-raising money, Walsh concentrated on the construction of a dormitory system. He built Freshman Hall in 1922, Sophomore Hall in 1923, and began construction of [Morrissey](/wiki/Morrissey_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Morrissey Hall (University of Notre Dame)"), [Howard](/wiki/Howard_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Howard Hall (University of Notre Dame)") and [Lyons](/wiki/Lyons_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Lyons Hall (University of Notre Dame)") Halls between 1924 and 1925.[[43]](#cite_note-44) By 1925, enrollment had increased to 2,500 students, of which 1,471 lived on campus; faculty members increased from 90 to 175. On the academic side, credit hours were reduced to encourage in-depth study, and Latin and Greek were no longer required. In 1928, three years of college were made a prerequisite for the study of law.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Walsh expanded the College of Commerce, enlarged the stadium, completed South Dining Hall, and built the memorial and entrance transept of the [Basilica](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart,_Notre_Dame "Basilica of the Sacred Heart, Notre Dame").[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

One of the main driving forces in the university's growth was its football team, the Notre Dame Fighting Irish.[[47]](#cite_note-ncronline.org-48) [Knute Rockne](/wiki/Knute_Rockne "Knute Rockne") became head coach in 1918. Under him, the Irish won three national championships, had five undefeated seasons, won the [Rose Bowl Game](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_Game "Rose Bowl Game") in 1925, and produced players such as [George Gipp](/wiki/George_Gipp "George Gipp") and the "[Four Horsemen](/wiki/Four_Horsemen_(American_football) "Four Horsemen (American football)")". Knute Rockne has the highest winning percentage (.881) in NCAA Division I/FBS football history. Rockne's offenses employed the Notre Dame Box and his defenses ran a [7–2–2 scheme](/wiki/7-2-2_defense "7-2-2 defense").[[48]](#cite_note-49)

The success of Notre Dame reflected the rising status of Irish Americans and Catholics in the 1920s. Catholics rallied around the team and listened to the games on the radio, especially when it defeated teams from schools that symbolized the Protestant establishment in the United States — Harvard, Yale, Princeton, and Army.[[47]](#cite_note-ncronline.org-48) Its role as a high-profile flagship institution of Catholicism made it an easy target of [anti-Catholicism](/wiki/Anti-Catholicism "Anti-Catholicism"). The most remarkable episode of violence was a clash in 1924 between Notre Dame students and the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") (KKK), a [white supremacist](/wiki/White_supremacist "White supremacist") and [anti-Catholic](/wiki/Anti-Catholic "Anti-Catholic") movement. The Klan decided to hold a week-long Klavern in South Bend. Clashes with the student body started on May 17 when students blocked the Klansmen from descending from their trains in the South Bend station and ripped KKK clothes and regalia. Two days later, thousands of students massed downtown protesting the Klavern, and only the arrival of college president Walsh prevented any further clashes. The next day, Rockne spoke at a campus rally and implored the students to refrain from further violence. A few days later, the Klavern broke up, but the hostility shown by the students contributed to the downfall of the KKK in Indiana.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)

### Expansion in the 1930s and 1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Expansion in the 1930s and 1940s")]

[![View of both Alumni and Dillon Hall on Notre Dame's South Quad.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/South_Quad%2C_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG/250px-South_Quad%2C_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG)](/wiki/File:South_Quad,_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG)

South Quad, built in the 1920s–1940s, includes many residential halls

[Charles L. O'Donnell](/wiki/Charles_L._O%27Donnell "Charles L. O'Donnell") (1928–1934) and [John Francis O'Hara](/wiki/John_Francis_O%27Hara "John Francis O'Hara") (1934–1939) fueled both material and academic expansion. During their tenures at Notre Dame, they brought many refugees and intellectuals to campus, such as [W. B. Yeats](/wiki/W._B._Yeats "W. B. Yeats"), [Frank H. Spearman](/wiki/Frank_H._Spearman "Frank H. Spearman"), [Jeremiah D. M. Ford](/wiki/Jeremiah_D._M._Ford "Jeremiah D. M. Ford"), [Irvin Abell](/wiki/Irvin_Abell "Irvin Abell"), and Josephine Brownson for the Laetare Medal, instituted in 1883. O'Hara also concentrated on expanding the graduate school.[[51]](#cite_note-52) New construction included [Notre Dame Stadium](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Stadium "Notre Dame Stadium"), the law school building, the Rockne Memorial, numerous residential halls, the Cushing Hall of Engineering, and a new heating plant. This rapid expansion, which cost the university more than $2.8 million, was made possible in large part through football revenues. O'Hara strongly believed that the Fighting Irish football team could be an effective means to "acquaint the public with the ideals that dominate" Notre Dame. He wrote, "Notre Dame football is a spiritual service because it is played for the honor and glory of God and of his Blessed Mother. When St. Paul said: 'Whether you eat or drink, or whatsoever else you do, do all for the glory of God,' he included football."[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESperber2002400-53)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), O'Donnell offered Notre Dame's facilities to the armed forces. The Navy accepted his offer and installed Naval [Reserve Officers' Training Corps](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps") (ROTC) units on campus as part of the [V-12 Navy College Training Program](/wiki/V-12_Navy_College_Training_Program "V-12 Navy College Training Program").[[53]](#cite_note-54) Soon after the installation, there were only a few hundred civilian students at Notre Dame. O'Donnell continued O'Hara's work with the graduate school. He formalized the graduate program further and replaced the previous committee of graduate studies with a dean.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

[John J. Cavanaugh](/wiki/John_J._Cavanaugh "John J. Cavanaugh"), president from 1946 to 1952, devoted his efforts to raising academic standards and reshaping the university administration to serve better its educational mission and an expanded student body. He stressed advanced studies and research while quadrupling the university's student population, with undergraduate enrollment increasing by more than half and graduate student enrollment growing fivefold. Cavanaugh established the Lobund Institute for Animal Studies and Notre Dame's [Medieval Institute](/wiki/Notre_Dame_College_of_Arts_and_Letters#Medieval_Institute "Notre Dame College of Arts and Letters"),[[55]](#cite_note-56) presided over the construction of Nieuwland Science Hall, Fisher Hall, and the Morris Inn, and the Hall of [Liberal Arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts "Liberal arts") (now O'Shaughnessy Hall), made possible by a donation from I. A. O'Shaughnessy, at the time the largest ever made to an American Catholic university.[[56]](#cite_note-57) He also established the university's system of advisory councils.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### Hesburgh era: 1952–1987

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hesburgh era: 1952–1987")]

[![The Word of Life, a large mural on the side of the Theodore Hesburgh Library depicting the resurrected Jesus](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Touchdown_Jesus_at_Notre_Dame.jpg/250px-Touchdown_Jesus_at_Notre_Dame.jpg)](/wiki/File:Touchdown_Jesus_at_Notre_Dame.jpg)

The [Hesburgh Library](/wiki/Hesburgh_Library "Hesburgh Library"), decorated by the *[Word of Life](/wiki/Word_of_Life_(mural) "Word of Life (mural)")* mural.

[Theodore Hesburgh](/wiki/Theodore_Hesburgh "Theodore Hesburgh") served as president for 35 years (1952–1987). Under his presidency, Notre Dame underwent huge growth and transformation from a school mostly known for its football to a top-tier university, academic powerhouse, and preeminent Catholic university.[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) The annual operating budget rose by a factor of 18, from $9.7 million to $176.6 million; the endowment by a factor of 40, from $9 million to $350 million; and research funding by a factor of 20, from $735,000 to $15 million. Enrollment nearly doubled from 4,979 to 9,600; faculty more than doubled from 389 to 950, and degrees awarded annually doubled from 1,212 to 2,500.[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65)

Hesburgh made Notre Dame coeducational. Women had graduated every year since 1917, but they were mostly religious sisters in graduate programs.[[65]](#cite_note-66) In the mid-1960s, Notre Dame and Saint Mary's College developed a co-exchange program whereby several hundred students took classes not offered at their home institution. After extensive debate, merging with St. Mary's was rejected, primarily because of the different faculty qualifications and pay scales. "In American college education," explained [Charles E. Sheedy](/wiki/Charles_E._Sheedy "Charles E. Sheedy"), Notre Dame's dean of Arts and Letters, "certain features formerly considered advantageous and enviable are now seen as anachronistic and out of place. ... In this environment of diversity, the integration of the sexes is a normal and expected aspect, replacing separatism."[[66]](#cite_note-67) Two of the residence halls were converted for the newly admitted female students that first year,[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69) with two more converted the next school year.[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71) In 1971, Mary Ann Proctor, a transfer from St. Mary's, became the first female undergraduate. The following year, Mary Davey Bliley became the first woman to graduate from the university with a bachelor's degree in marketing.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73) In 1978, a [historic district](/wiki/Historic_district_(United_States) "Historic district (United States)") comprising 21 contributing buildings was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[73]](#cite_note-nrhpdoc-ndc-74)

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Recent history")]

In the eighteen years [Edward Malloy](/wiki/Edward_Malloy "Edward Malloy") was president, the school's reputation, faculty, and resources grew rapidly.[[74]](#cite_note-Notre_Dame's_president_to_retire-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77) He added more than 500 professors and the academic quality of the student body improved dramatically, with the average [Scholastic Assessment Test](/wiki/Scholastic_Assessment_Test "Scholastic Assessment Test") (SAT) score rising from 1240 to 1460. The number of minority students more than doubled, the endowment grew from $350 million to more than $3 billion, the annual operating budget rose from $177 million to more than $650 million, and annual research funding improved from $15 million to more than $70 million.[[74]](#cite_note-Notre_Dame's_president_to_retire-75) Notre Dame's most recent (2014) capital campaign raised $2.014 billion, far exceeding its goal of $767 million. It was the largest in the history of Catholic higher education, and the largest of any university without a medical school at the time.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

[John I. Jenkins](/wiki/John_I._Jenkins "John I. Jenkins") took over from Malloy in 2005.[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80) In his inaugural address, Jenkins described his goals of making the university a leader in research that recognizes ethics and builds the connection between faith and studies. During his tenure, Notre Dame has increased its endowment, enlarged its student body, and undergone many construction projects on campus, including the [Compton Family Ice Arena](/wiki/Compton_Family_Ice_Arena "Compton Family Ice Arena"), a new architecture hall, and additional residence halls.[[80]](#cite_note-81) Announced as an integration of "the academy, student life and athletics,"[[81]](#cite_note-82) construction on the 750,000 sq ft (70,000 m2) [Campus Crossroads](/wiki/Campus_Crossroads "Campus Crossroads") project began around Notre Dame Stadium in November 2014. Its three buildings house student life services, an indoor gym, a recreation center, the career center, a 500-seat student ballroom, the departments of anthropology and psychology, a digital media center and the department of music and sacred music program.[[82]](#cite_note-2018-01-16SBendTr-83)

Jenkins announced the 2023–2024 academic year would be his last as president in October 2023. The board of trustees subsequently elected [Robert A. Dowd](/wiki/Robert_A._Dowd "Robert A. Dowd") to succeed him, effective June 1, 2024.[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Campus")]

Main article: [Campus of the University of Notre Dame](/wiki/Campus_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame "Campus of the University of Notre Dame")

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Notre_Dame_campus_view.jpg/250px-Notre_Dame_campus_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Notre_Dame_campus_view.jpg)

A view of the historical part of the campus, with the Basilica and Main Building

Notre Dame's campus is located in [Notre Dame, Indiana](/wiki/Notre_Dame,_Indiana "Notre Dame, Indiana"), in the [Michiana](/wiki/Michiana "Michiana") area of [Northern Indiana](/wiki/Northern_Indiana "Northern Indiana").[[85]](#cite_note-About_ND-86) It lies on 1,250 acres (510 ha), south of the [Indiana Toll Road](/wiki/Indiana_Toll_Road "Indiana Toll Road") and includes around 170 buildings and athletic fields located around its two lakes and seven [quadrangles](/wiki/Quadrangle_(architecture) "Quadrangle (architecture)").[[86]](#cite_note-Campus-87)

It is consistently ranked as one of the most beautiful university campuses in the United States and around the world, and it is noted particularly for the Golden Dome, the Basilica and its stained glass windows, the quads and the greenery, the Grotto, the [Hesburgh Library](/wiki/Hesburgh_Library "Hesburgh Library") with its Word of Life stone mural (nicknamed "Touchdown Jesus" by students), and its statues and museums.[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93) Notre Dame is a major tourist attraction in northern Indiana; in the 2015–2016 academic year, more than 1.8 million visitors, almost half of whom were from outside [St. Joseph County](/wiki/St._Joseph_County,_Indiana "St. Joseph County, Indiana"), visited the campus.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

A 116-acre (47 ha) historic district was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978 as University of Notre Dame: Main and South Quadrangles. The district includes 21 [contributing buildings](/wiki/Contributing_property "Contributing property") in the core of the original campus such as the Main Administration Building and the Basilica.[[73]](#cite_note-nrhpdoc-ndc-74)

### Administration and academic buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Administration and academic buildings")]

The Main Building serves as the center for the university's administrative offices, including the Office of the President. Its golden dome, topped by the statue of Mary, is the campus' most recognizable landmark. The main building is located on Main Quad (also known as "God Quad"), which is the oldest, most historic, and most central part of campus. Behind the main building stands several facilities with administrative purposes and student services, including Carole Sadner Hall, Brownson Hall, and St. Liam's Hall, the campus health center.

There are several religious buildings.[[94]](#cite_note-95) The current [Basilica of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart_(Notre_Dame) "Basilica of the Sacred Heart (Notre Dame)") is on the site of Sorin's original church, which had become too small for the growing college. It is built in French Revival style, with stained glass windows imported from France. [Luigi Gregori](/wiki/Luigi_Gregori "Luigi Gregori"), an Italian painter invited by Sorin to be an artist in residence, painted the interior. The basilica also features a bell tower with a carillon. Inside the church, there are sculptures by [Ivan Meštrović](/wiki/Ivan_Me%C5%A1trovi%C4%87 "Ivan Meštrović"). The Grotto of Our Lady of Lourdes, built in 1896, is a replica of the [original](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Our_Lady_of_Lourdes "Sanctuary of Our Lady of Lourdes") in [Lourdes](/wiki/Lourdes "Lourdes") and is a popular spot for prayer and meditation.[[95]](#cite_note-96) The Old College building has become one of two [seminaries](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") on the campus run by the Congregation of Holy Cross.

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/NDU-Goldkuppel.jpg/250px-NDU-Goldkuppel.jpg)](/wiki/File:NDU-Goldkuppel.jpg)

The Golden Dome, built under Sorin, has become the symbol of the university

Academic buildings are concentrated in the Center-South and Center-East sections of campus. McCourtney Hall, an interdisciplinary research facility, opened its doors for the fall 2016 semester, and ground was broken on the 60,000 sq ft (5,600 m2) [Walsh Family Hall of Architecture](/wiki/Walsh_Family_Hall_of_Architecture "Walsh Family Hall of Architecture") on the south end of campus near the DeBartolo Performing Arts Center which opened in fall 2018.[[96]](#cite_note-97) Since 2004, several buildings have been added, including the DeBartolo Performing Arts Center,[[97]](#cite_note-DPAC-98) the Guglielmino Complex,[[98]](#cite_note-99) and the Jordan Hall of Science.[[99]](#cite_note-100) A new engineering building, Stinson-Remick Hall, a new combination Center for Social Concerns/Institute for Church Life building, Geddes Hall, and a law school addition were completed at the same time.[[100]](#cite_note-101) Many academic buildings were built with a system of libraries, the most prominent of which is the Hesburgh Library, built in 1963 and today containing almost four million books. The Stayer Center for Executive Education, which houses the Mendoza College of Business Executive Education Department, opened in March 2013, just south of the Mendoza College of Business building.

### Residential and student buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Residential and student buildings")]

Main article: [University of Notre Dame residence halls](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame_residence_halls "University of Notre Dame residence halls")

There are [33 single-sex undergraduate residence halls](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame_residence_halls "University of Notre Dame residence halls"). The university has recently announced a co-educational undergraduate dorm community based in one of the graduate residential apartments.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Most of the graduate students on campus live in one of four graduate housing complexes on campus. A new residence for men, [Baumer Hall](/wiki/Baumer_Hall "Baumer Hall"), was built in 2019. [Johnson Family Hall](/wiki/Johnson_Family_Hall "Johnson Family Hall"), for women, was also completed and opened that semester. The South Dining Hall and North Dining Hall serve the student body.

[![Colorful trees on the Main and South Quadrangles in autumn](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Fall_on_%22God_Quad%22_that_Connects_North_Quad_and_South_Quad.jpg/250px-Fall_on_%22God_Quad%22_that_Connects_North_Quad_and_South_Quad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fall_on_%22God_Quad%22_that_Connects_North_Quad_and_South_Quad.jpg)

Fall on the Main Quadrangle

The campus hosts several entertainment, general purpose, and common spaces. LaFortune Student Center, commonly known as "LaFortune" or "LaFun," is a four-story building built in 1883 that serves the student union and hosts social, recreational, cultural, and educational activities.[[102]](#cite_note-Lafortune_Student_Center-103)[[103]](#cite_note-acui.org-104) LaFortune hosts many businesses (including restaurant chains), student services, and divisions of The Office of Student Affairs.[[102]](#cite_note-Lafortune_Student_Center-103)[[104]](#cite_note-105) A second student union came with the addition of Duncan Student Center, which is built onto the Notre Dame Stadium as part of the Campus Crossroads projects. As well as additional food service chains, recreation facilities, and student offices, Duncan also hosts a student gym and a ballroom.

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/University_of_Notre_Dame%27s_God_Quad.JPG/250px-University_of_Notre_Dame%27s_God_Quad.JPG)](/wiki/File:University_of_Notre_Dame%27s_God_Quad.JPG)

Historic Washington Hall on the Main Quadrangle, popularly termed the "God Quad"[[105]](#cite_note-106)

### Athletics facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Athletics facilities")]

Because of its long athletic tradition, the university features many athletic buildings, which are concentrated in the southern and eastern sections of campus. The most prominent is [Notre Dame Stadium](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Stadium "Notre Dame Stadium"),[[106]](#cite_note-107) home of the [Fighting Irish football team](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football"); it has been renovated several times and today can seat over 80,000 people. Prominent venues include the [Edmund P. Joyce Center](/wiki/Edmund_P._Joyce_Center "Edmund P. Joyce Center"), with indoor basketball and volleyball courts, and the [Compton Family Ice Arena](/wiki/Compton_Family_Ice_Arena "Compton Family Ice Arena"),[[107]](#cite_note-108) a two-rink facility dedicated to hockey. There are many outdoor fields, such as the [Frank Eck Stadium](/wiki/Frank_Eck_Stadium "Frank Eck Stadium") for baseball.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

[Legends of Notre Dame](/wiki/Legends_of_Notre_Dame "Legends of Notre Dame") (commonly called Legends) is a [music venue](/wiki/Music_venue "Music venue"), [public house](/wiki/Public_house "Public house"), and restaurant on campus, just 100 yd (91 m) south of the stadium. The former Alumni Senior Club[[109]](#cite_note-SAO_Website-110) opened in September 2003 after a $3.5 million renovation and became an all-ages student hang-out. Legends is made up of two parts: The Restaurant and Alehouse and the nightclub.[[110]](#cite_note-Legends_Observer_Article-111)

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Law_Arch_3.JPG/250px-Law_Arch_3.JPG)](/wiki/File:Law_Arch_3.JPG)

The new wing of the [Law School](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Law_School "Notre Dame Law School")

### Environmental sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Environmental sustainability")]

The Office of Sustainability was created in the fall of 2007 at the recommendation of a Sustainability Strategy Working Group and appointed the first director in April 2008. The pursuit of [sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") is related directly to the Catholic mission of the university.[[111]](#cite_note-112) Other resources and centers on campus focusing on sustainability include the Environmental Change Initiative, Environmental Research Center, and the Center for Sustainable Energy at Notre Dame.[[112]](#cite_note-Dame-113) The university also houses the Kellogg Institute for International Peace Studies.

Notre Dame received a gold rating from the [Association for the Advancement of Sustainability in Higher Education](/wiki/Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Sustainability_in_Higher_Education "Association for the Advancement of Sustainability in Higher Education") (AASHE) in 2014, though in 2017 it was downgraded to silver.[[113]](#cite_note-114) In 2016, the Office of Sustainability released its Comprehensive Sustainability Strategy to achieve its goals in a wide area of university operations.[[114]](#cite_note-About_the_Office//Office_of_Sustainability//University_of_Notre_Dame-115) As of November 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit), 17 buildings have achieved [LEED-Certified status](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design#Certification_level "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design"), with 12 of them earning Gold certification.[[115]](#cite_note-116) Notre Dame's dining service sources 40 percent of its food locally and offers sustainably caught seafood and many organic, fair-trade, and vegan options.[[116]](#cite_note-Design_&_Construction//Office_of_Sustainability//University_of_Notre_Dame-117) In 2019, irrigation systems' improvements led to 244 million fewer gallons of water being used and a 50 percent reduction in water consumption over 10 years.[[117]](#cite_note-118)

In 2015, Notre Dame announced major environmental sustainability goals, including eliminating using coal by 2020 and reducing its carbon footprint by half by 2030.[[112]](#cite_note-Dame-113) Both these goals were reached in early 2019.[[118]](#cite_note-McLaughlin-119)[[119]](#cite_note-green.nd.edu-120) This was achieved by implementing energy conservation, energy efficiency strategies, temperature setpoints, low-flow water devices, and diversifying its energy sources and infrastructures.[[118]](#cite_note-McLaughlin-119) New sources of renewable energy on campus include [geothermal wells](/wiki/Geothermal_heat_pump "Geothermal heat pump") on East Quad and by the Notre Dame Stadium, substitution of boilers with gas turbines, solar panels on Fitzpatrick Hall and Stinson-Remick Hall and off-campus, a [hydroelectric](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") facility at Seitz Park in South Bend powered by the [St. Joseph River](/wiki/St._Joseph_River_(Lake_Michigan) "St. Joseph River (Lake Michigan)"), and heat recovery strategies.[[120]](#cite_note-121)[[119]](#cite_note-green.nd.edu-120)[[112]](#cite_note-Dame-113) Future projects outlined by the university's utilities long-range plan include continual diversification of its energy portfolio, future geothermal wells in new buildings and some existing facilities, and a collaboration with the South Bend Solar Project. Current goals include cutting Notre Dame's carbon footprint by 83 percent by 2050 and eventually becoming carbon neutral, diverting 67 percent of all waste from landfills by 2030.[[112]](#cite_note-Dame-113)[[119]](#cite_note-green.nd.edu-120)[[118]](#cite_note-McLaughlin-119)

### Global Gateways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Global Gateways")]

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Former_United_University_Club.jpg/250px-Former_United_University_Club.jpg)](/wiki/File:Former_United_University_Club.jpg)

The former [United University Club](/wiki/United_University_Club "United University Club")

The university owns several centers around the world used for international studies and research, conferences abroad, and alumni support.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

* *London*. The university has had a presence in London since 1968. Since 1998, its London center has been based in Fischer Hall, the former [United University Club](/wiki/United_University_Club "United University Club") in Trafalgar Square. The center hosts the university's programs in the city, and conferences and symposia.[[122]](#cite_note-123) The university also owns a residence facility, [Conway Hall](/wiki/Royal_Waterloo_Hospital_for_Children_and_Women "Royal Waterloo Hospital for Children and Women"), for students studying abroad.[[123]](#cite_note-124)
* *Beijing*. The university owns space in the [Liangmaqiao Station](/wiki/Liangmaqiao_Station "Liangmaqiao Station") area. The center is the hub of Notre Dame Asia. It hosts a number of programs including study abroad.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Kylemore_Abbey2007.jpg/250px-Kylemore_Abbey2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kylemore_Abbey2007.jpg)

[Kylemore Abbey](/wiki/Kylemore_Abbey "Kylemore Abbey")

* *[Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin")*. The university owns the O'Connell House, a building in [Merrion Square](/wiki/Merrion_Square "Merrion Square") in the heart of [Georgian Dublin](/wiki/Georgian_Dublin "Georgian Dublin"). It hosts academic programs and summer internships in [Irish studies](/wiki/Irish_studies "Irish studies") for both undergraduate and graduate students in addition to seminars, and is home to the Keough Naughton Centre.[[125]](#cite_note-126) Since 2015, the university has partnered with [Kylemore Abbey](/wiki/Kylemore_Abbey "Kylemore Abbey"), renovating spaces in the abbey so it could host academic programs.[[126]](#cite_note-127)
* *[Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem")*. The Jerusalem Global Gateway shares space with the university's [Tantur Ecumenical Institute](/wiki/Tantur_Ecumenical_Institute "Tantur Ecumenical Institute"), in a 100,000 sq ft (9,300 m2) facility on the seam between Jerusalem and [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"). It hosts religious and ecumenical programs.[[127]](#cite_note-128)
* *Rome*. The Rome Global Gateway is on Via Ostilia, very close to the [Colosseum](/wiki/Colosseum "Colosseum"). It was recently acquired and renovated and now has 32,000 sq ft (3,000 m2) of space to host a variety of academic activities. The university purchased a second Roman villa on the Caelian hill.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

In addition to the five Global Gateways, the university also owns the [Santa Fe Building](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Building_(Chicago) "Santa Fe Building (Chicago)") in Chicago, where it offers its executive [Master of Business Administration](/wiki/Master_of_Business_Administration "Master of Business Administration") program.[[129]](#cite_note-130) The university also hosts Global Centers located in [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago "Santiago"), [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai").

### Community development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Community development")]

The first phase of Eddy Street Commons, a $215 million development adjacent to campus funded by the university, broke ground in June 2008.[[130]](#cite_note-131)[[131]](#cite_note-132) The project drew union protests when workers hired by the City of South Bend to construct the public parking garage picketed the private work site after a contractor hired non-union workers.[[132]](#cite_note-133) The $90 million second phase broke ground in 2017.[[133]](#cite_note-134)

## Organization and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Organization and administration")]

See also: [President of the University of Notre Dame](/wiki/President_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame "President of the University of Notre Dame")

[![Hesburgh sitting at his desk](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Fr._Ted_Hesburgh_in_his_Office_at_the_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG/250px-Fr._Ted_Hesburgh_in_his_Office_at_the_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fr._Ted_Hesburgh_in_his_Office_at_the_University_of_Notre_Dame.JPG)

[Theodore Hesburgh](/wiki/Theodore_Hesburgh "Theodore Hesburgh"), photographed in 2012, was the 15th and longest-serving president of Notre Dame.

[The university's president](/wiki/President_of_the_University_of_Notre_Dame "President of the University of Notre Dame") is always a priest of the Congregation of Holy Cross. The first president was [Edward Sorin](/wiki/Edward_Sorin "Edward Sorin"); and the current president is [Robert A. Dowd](/wiki/Robert_A._Dowd "Robert A. Dowd"). As of June 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit), [John McGreevy](/wiki/John_McGreevy "John McGreevy") is the [provost](/wiki/Provost_(education) "Provost (education)") overseeing academic functions.[[134]](#cite_note-135)
Until 1967, Notre Dame had been governed directly by the Congregation. Under the presidency of [Theodore Hesburgh](/wiki/Theodore_Hesburgh "Theodore Hesburgh"), two groups, the Board of Fellows, and the [Board of Trustees](/wiki/Trustee "Trustee"), were established to govern the university.[[135]](#cite_note-leadership-136) The 12 fellows are evenly divided between members of the Holy Cross order and the laity; they have final say over the operation of the university. They vote on potential trustees and sign off on all that board's major decisions.[[136]](#cite_note-137) The trustees elect the president and provide general guidance and governance to the university.[[135]](#cite_note-leadership-136)

### Endowment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Endowment")]

Notre Dame's [endowment](/wiki/Financial_endowment "Financial endowment") was started in the early 1920s by university president James Burns; it was $7 million by 1952 when Hesburgh became president. For fiscal year 2023, the university reported total endowment assets of $16.62 billion.[[137]](#cite_note-NACUBO-138)[[138]](#cite_note-AnnualReport-139)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Academics")]

### Colleges and schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Colleges and schools")]

* The [College of Arts and Letters](/wiki/Notre_Dame_College_of_Arts_and_Letters "Notre Dame College of Arts and Letters") was established as the university's first college in 1842. The first degrees were granted seven years later.[[139]](#cite_note-Hope_5-140) The university's first academic curriculum was modeled after the [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") *[Ratio Studiorum](/wiki/Ratio_Studiorum "Ratio Studiorum")* from [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University "Saint Louis University").[[140]](#cite_note-141) Today, the college, housed in O'Shaughnessy Hall,[[141]](#cite_note-142) includes 20 departments in the areas of fine arts, humanities, and social sciences, and awards Bachelor of Arts (B.A.) degrees in nearly 70 majors and minors, making it the largest of the university's colleges. As of 2022, there were 2,000 undergraduates and graduates enrolled in the college, taught by 500 faculty members.[[142]](#cite_note-143)

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Jordan_Hall.jpg/250px-Jordan_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jordan_Hall.jpg)

Jordan Hall of Science

* The [College of Science](/wiki/Notre_Dame_College_of_Science "Notre Dame College of Science") was established in 1865. The curriculum involved six years of coursework, including higher-level mathematics.[[143]](#cite_note-144) Today, the college, housed in the Jordan Hall of Science,[[144]](#cite_note-145) includes over 1,200 undergraduates in several departments, each awarding Bachelor of Science (B.S.) degrees.[[145]](#cite_note-146) According to university statistics, its science pre-professional program has one of the highest acceptance rates to medical school of any university in the United States.[[146]](#cite_note-147)
* The [Notre Dame Law School](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Law_School "Notre Dame Law School") was established in 1869 and is the oldest [law program](/wiki/Law_school_in_the_United_States "Law school in the United States") at a Catholic university in the United States.[[147]](#cite_note-Law-148) In 2024, the school [ranked](/wiki/Law_school_rankings_in_the_United_States "Law school rankings in the United States") 20th among the top American law schools by *U.S. News & World Report*.[[148]](#cite_note-149) The law school grants the professional [Juris Doctor](/wiki/Juris_Doctor "Juris Doctor") degree, as well as the graduate [Master of Laws](/wiki/Master_of_Laws "Master of Laws"), and [Doctor of Juridical Science](/wiki/Doctor_of_Juridical_Science "Doctor of Juridical Science") degrees.[[149]](#cite_note-Grad_Degrees-150) It was ranked fourth in graduates attaining federal judicial [clerkships](/wiki/Law_clerk "Law clerk") in 2024,[[150]](#cite_note-151) and seventh in graduates attaining [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") clerkships in 2020.[[151]](#cite_note-152)

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Bond_Hall-_University_of_Notre_Dame-_School_of_Architecture.JPG/250px-Bond_Hall-_University_of_Notre_Dame-_School_of_Architecture.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bond_Hall-_University_of_Notre_Dame-_School_of_Architecture.JPG)

[Bond Hall](/wiki/Bond_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Bond Hall (University of Notre Dame)"), house of the School of Architecture from 1964 until 2019

* The [School of Architecture](/wiki/Notre_Dame_School_of_Architecture "Notre Dame School of Architecture") was established in 1899,[[152]](#cite_note-153) the year after Notre Dame first granted degrees in the field.[[153]](#cite_note-154) Today, the school, housed in [Walsh Family Hall of Architecture](/wiki/Walsh_Family_Hall_of_Architecture "Walsh Family Hall of Architecture"),[[154]](#cite_note-155) offers a five-year undergraduate program leading to a bachelor's degree. All undergraduates spend their third year in Rome.[[155]](#cite_note-156) The faculty teaches ([pre-modernist](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture")) [traditional](/wiki/Vernacular_architecture "Vernacular architecture") and [classical](/wiki/Classical_architecture "Classical architecture") architecture and [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning") (e.g., following the principles of [New Urbanism](/wiki/New_Urbanism "New Urbanism") and [New Classical Architecture](/wiki/New_Classical_Architecture "New Classical Architecture")).[[156]](#cite_note-157) It also awards the annual [Driehaus Architecture Prize](/wiki/Driehaus_Architecture_Prize "Driehaus Architecture Prize").[[157]](#cite_note-158)
* The [College of Engineering](/wiki/Notre_Dame_College_of_Engineering "Notre Dame College of Engineering") was established in 1920;[[158]](#cite_note-159) however, courses in [civil](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering") and mechanical engineering had been taught in the College of Science, since the 1870s.[[159]](#cite_note-160) Today, the college, housed in the Fitzpatrick, Cushing, and Stinson-Remick Halls of Engineering,[[160]](#cite_note-161) includes five departments of study, with eight B.S. degrees offered. The college also offers five-year dual degree programs with the College of Arts and Letters and the College of Business awarding additional B.A. and [Master of Business Administration](/wiki/Master_of_Business_Administration "Master of Business Administration") (MBA) degrees, respectively.[[161]](#cite_note-162)
* The [Mendoza College of Business](/wiki/Mendoza_College_of_Business "Mendoza College of Business") was established in 1921 by [John Francis O'Hara](/wiki/John_Francis_O%27Hara "John Francis O'Hara"), although a foreign commerce program had been launched in 1917.[[162]](#cite_note-163) Today, the college offers degrees in accountancy, finance, management, and marketing and enrolls over 1,600 students.[[163]](#cite_note-164) In 2016, *[Bloomberg Businessweek](/wiki/Bloomberg_Businessweek "Bloomberg Businessweek")* ranked Mendoza's undergraduate program as second in the country,[[164]](#cite_note-165) after five consecutive years in the first position.[[165]](#cite_note-166) For its 2023 ranking, *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* ranked the graduate school 25th, tied with [Vanderbilt University](/wiki/Vanderbilt_University "Vanderbilt University").[[166]](#cite_note-167)
* The [Keough School of Global Affairs](/wiki/Keough_School_of_Global_Affairs "Keough School of Global Affairs") was established in 2014. The first new school in nearly a century, it builds on the presence of seven institutes founded for international research, scholarship, and education at Notre Dame. The school offers six doctoral programs related to international peace studies, a Masters in Global Affairs focused on either peace studies or sustainable development, and five undergraduate majors.[[167]](#cite_note-168) It is focused on the study of global governance, human rights, and other areas of global social and political policy. A $50 million gift from [Donald Keough](/wiki/Donald_Keough "Donald Keough"), a former [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola") executive, and his wife Marilyn funded the school's creation. The school opened officially in August 2017, in Jenkins Hall on Debartolo Quad.[[168]](#cite_note-169)

#### Special programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Special programs")]

Every Notre Dame undergraduate is part of one of the school's five undergraduate colleges or is in the First Year of Studies program.[[169]](#cite_note-170) The First Year of Studies program was established in 1962 to guide [freshmen](/wiki/First_year "First year") through their first year at the school before they have declared a [major](/wiki/Major_(academic) "Major (academic)"). Each student is assigned an academic advisor who helps them choose classes that give them exposure to any major in which they are interested.[[170]](#cite_note-171) The program includes a Learning Resource Center, which provides time management, collaborative learning, and subject tutoring.[[171]](#cite_note-172) First Year of Studies is designed to encourage intellectual and academic achievement and innovation among first-year students. It includes programs such as FY advising, the Dean's A-list, the Renaissance circle, NDignite, the First Year Urban challenge, and more.[[172]](#cite_note-173) Every admissions cycle, the Office of Undergraduate Admissions selects a small number of students for the Glynn Family Honors Program, which grants top students within the College of Arts and Letters and the College of Science access to smaller class sizes taught by distinguished faculty, endowed funding for independent research, and dedicated advising faculty and staff.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

### Graduate education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Graduate education")]

Each college offers graduate education in the form of master's and doctoral programs. Most of the departments in the College of Arts and Letters offer PhDs, while a professional [Master of Divinity](/wiki/Master_of_Divinity "Master of Divinity") (M.Div.) program also exists. All of the departments in the College of Science offer PhDs, except for the Department of Pre-Professional Studies. The School of Architecture offers a [Master of Architecture](/wiki/Master_of_Architecture "Master of Architecture"), while each of the departments of the College of Engineering offer PhDs. The College of Business offers multiple professional programs, including MBA and [Master of Science in Accountancy](/wiki/Master_of_Accountancy "Master of Accountancy") programs. It also operates facilities in Chicago and Cincinnati for its executive MBA program.[[149]](#cite_note-Grad_Degrees-150) The Alliance for Catholic Education program[[174]](#cite_note-175) offers a [Master of Education](/wiki/Master_of_Education "Master of Education") program, where students study at the university during the summer and teach in Catholic elementary schools, middle schools, and high schools across the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") for two school years.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

The university first offered graduate degrees, in the form of a Master of Arts (MA), in the 1854–1855 academic year. The program expanded to include [Master of Laws](/wiki/Master_of_Laws "Master of Laws") (LLM) and Master of Civil Engineering in its early stages of growth, before a formal graduate school education was developed with a [thesis](/wiki/Dissertation "Dissertation") not required to receive the degrees. This changed in 1924, with formal requirements developed for graduate degrees, including offering doctorates.[[176]](#cite_note-177) Although Notre Dame does not have its own medical school, it offers a combined [MD–PhD](/wiki/MD%E2%80%93PhD "MD–PhD") though the regional campus of the [Indiana University School of Medicine](/wiki/Indiana_University_School_of_Medicine "Indiana University School of Medicine"), where Indiana medical students may spend the first two years of their medical education before transferring to the main medical campus at [Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis](/wiki/Indiana_University%E2%80%93Purdue_University_Indianapolis "Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis").[[177]](#cite_note-178)[[178]](#cite_note-179)

### Centers and institutes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Centers and institutes")]

In 2019, Notre Dame announced plans to rename the Center for Ethics and Culture, an organization focused on spreading Catholic moral and intellectual traditions. A $10 million gift from Anthony and Christie de Nicola funded the new de Nicola Center for Ethics and Culture.[[179]](#cite_note-sbtribune-180)

The university is also home to the McGrath Institute for Church Life, which "partners with Catholic dioceses, parishes and schools to address pastoral challenges with theological depth and rigor".[[180]](#cite_note-181)

The Joan B. Kroc Institute for International Peace Studies, founded in 1986 by donations from [Joan B. Kroc](/wiki/Joan_B._Kroc "Joan B. Kroc"), the surviving spouse of [McDonald's](/wiki/McDonald%27s "McDonald's") owner [Ray Kroc](/wiki/Ray_Kroc "Ray Kroc"), and inspired by Father Hesburgh, is dedicated to research, education, and outreach on the causes of violent conflict and the conditions for sustainable peace. It offers Ph.D., master's, and undergraduate degrees in peace studies. It has contributed to international policy discussions about peace building practices.[[181]](#cite_note-182)

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Libraries")]

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/University_of_Notre_Dame_Law_Library.JPG/250px-University_of_Notre_Dame_Law_Library.JPG)](/wiki/File:University_of_Notre_Dame_Law_Library.JPG)

The interior of the Kresge Law Library at the [Notre Dame Law School](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Law_School "Notre Dame Law School")

The university's library system is divided among the main 14-story Theodore M. Hesburgh Library, and each of the colleges and schools. The Hesburgh Library, completed in 1963, is the third building to house the main collection.[[182]](#cite_note-183) The *[Word of Life](/wiki/Word_of_Life_(mural) "Word of Life (mural)")* [mural](/wiki/Mural "Mural") by [Millard Sheets](/wiki/Millard_Sheets "Millard Sheets"), popularly known as "Touchdown Jesus" because of its proximity to Notre Dame Stadium and Jesus' arms appearing to make the signal for a [touchdown](/wiki/Touchdown "Touchdown"), adorns the front of the library.[[183]](#cite_note-:1-184)[[184]](#cite_note-185)

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Clarke_Memorial_Fountain.jpg/250px-Clarke_Memorial_Fountain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clarke_Memorial_Fountain.jpg)

The [Clarke Memorial Fountain](/wiki/Clarke_Memorial_Fountain "Clarke Memorial Fountain"), a war memorial dedicated in 1986, known colloquially as "Stonehenge"[[185]](#cite_note-186)

The library system also includes branch libraries for Architecture, Chemistry and Physics, Engineering, Law, Music, and [Mathematics](/wiki/O%27Meara_Mathematics_Library "O'Meara Mathematics Library") and information centers in the Mendoza College of Business, the Kellogg Institute for International Studies, the Joan B. Kroc Institute for International Peace Studies, and a slide library in O'Shaughnessy Hall.[[186]](#cite_note-187) A theology library, opened in the fall of 2015 on the first floor of Stanford Hall, is the first branch of the library system to be housed in a dorm room. With over three million volumes, the library system was the single largest university library in the world at the time of completion.[[187]](#cite_note-188) It remains one of the hundred largest libraries in the country.[[188]](#cite_note-189)

### Admissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Admissions")]

| Undergraduate admissions statistics | |
| --- | --- |
| 2024 entering class[[189]](#cite_note-190)Change vs. 2019[[190]](#cite_note-191)   --- | |
| Admit rate | 11.1% (Neutral decrease −9.1) |
| [Yield rate](/wiki/Yield_(college_admissions) "Yield (college admissions)") | 62% (Increase +5.4) |
| Test scores [middle 50%](/wiki/Interquartile_range "Interquartile range") | |

| [ACT](/wiki/ACT_(test) "ACT (test)") Composite | 33–35 (Increase +1 median) |

The fall 2024 incoming class admitted 3,324 from a pool of 29,943 applicants for 11.1 percent acceptance rate.[[191]](#cite_note-admissions.nd.edu-192) The university practices a non-restrictive [early action](/wiki/Early_action "Early action") policy that allows admitted students to consider admission to Notre Dame and any other colleges that accepted them.[[192]](#cite_note-193) This process admitted 1,675 of the 9,683 (17 percent) who requested it.[[193]](#cite_note-194) Admission is [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") for domestic applicants.[[194]](#cite_note-195) Admitted students came from 1,311 high schools; the average student traveled over 750 mi (1,210 km) to Notre Dame. While all entering students begin in the College of the First Year of Studies, 26 percent have indicated they plan to study in the liberal arts or social sciences, 21 percent in engineering, 26 percent in business, 24 percent in science, and 3 percent in architecture.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

### Tuition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Tuition")]

Tuition for full-time students at the University of Notre Dame in 2023 is $62,693 a year.[[196]](#cite_note-University_of_Notre_Dame-197) Room and board is estimated to be an additional $17,378 a year for students who live in campus housing. Notre Dame is a private university, so it offers the same tuition for in-state and out-of-state students.[[196]](#cite_note-University_of_Notre_Dame-197) In 2026, the university announced that it would expand its financial aid program to cover full tuition for undergraduate students from families earning under $150,000 annually, beginning in the 2026–2027 academic year.[[197]](#cite_note-198)[[198]](#cite_note-199)

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Research")]

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/University_of_Notre_Dame_Hallway.JPG/250px-University_of_Notre_Dame_Hallway.JPG)](/wiki/File:University_of_Notre_Dame_Hallway.JPG)

Hallway within Hurley Hall

Joseph Carrier, director of the Science Museum and the library, was a professor of chemistry and physics until 1874. Carrier taught that scientific research and its promise for progress were not antagonistic to the ideals of intellectual and moral culture endorsed by the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). Notable researchers in the early history of the university include John Augustine Zahm, whose book *Evolution and Dogma* (1896) defended certain aspects of evolutionary theory as true;[[210]](#cite_note-211) [Albert Zahm](/wiki/Albert_Francis_Zahm "Albert Francis Zahm"), John's brother, who built an early [wind tunnel](/wiki/Wind_tunnel "Wind tunnel") to compare lift to drag of aeronautical models; Jerome Green, who became the first American to send a wireless message;[[211]](#cite_note-212) and [Julius Nieuwland](/wiki/Julius_Nieuwland "Julius Nieuwland"), who performed early work on basic reactions that were used to create [neoprene](/wiki/Neoprene "Neoprene").[[212]](#cite_note-213) The study of [nuclear physics](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics") at the university began with the building of a [nuclear accelerator](/wiki/Particle_accelerator "Particle accelerator") in 1936[[213]](#cite_note-214) and continues now partly through a partnership in the [Joint Institute for Nuclear Astrophysics](/wiki/Joint_Institute_for_Nuclear_Astrophysics "Joint Institute for Nuclear Astrophysics").[[214]](#cite_note-215)

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Mestrovic_Pieta.jpg/250px-Mestrovic_Pieta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mestrovic_Pieta.jpg)

The [Pietà](/wiki/Piet%C3%A0_(Mestrovic) "Pietà (Mestrovic)") by [Ivan Meštrović](/wiki/Ivan_Me%C5%A1trovi%C4%87 "Ivan Meštrović"), a European émigré

The rise of Hitler and other dictators in the 1930s forced many Catholic intellectuals to flee Europe; President John O'Hara brought many of them to Notre Dame. [Anton-Hermann Chroust](/wiki/Anton-Hermann_Chroust "Anton-Hermann Chroust"), in classics and law,[[215]](#cite_note-216) and Waldemar Gurian, a German Catholic intellectual of Jewish descent, came from Germany.[[216]](#cite_note-217) Ivan Meštrović, a renowned sculptor, brought Croat culture to campus.[[217]](#cite_note-218) [Yves Simon](/wiki/Yves_Simon_(philosopher) "Yves Simon (philosopher)") brought the insights of French studies in the Aristotelian-Thomistic tradition of philosophy to the university in the 1940s; his teacher, [Jacques Maritain](/wiki/Jacques_Maritain "Jacques Maritain"), was a frequent visitor to campus.[[218]](#cite_note-219) The exiles developed a distinctive emphasis on the evils of [totalitarianism](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism").[[219]](#cite_note-220)
[Richard T. Sullivan](/wiki/Richard_T._Sullivan "Richard T. Sullivan") taught English from 1936 to 1974 and published six novels, dozens of short stories, and other works.[[220]](#cite_note-221) [Frank O'Malley](/wiki/Frank_O%27Malley "Frank O'Malley") was an English professor during the 1930s through the 1960s who developed a concept of Christian philosophy that was a fundamental element in his thought.[[221]](#cite_note-222) In 1939, [Waldemar Gurian](/wiki/Waldemar_Gurian "Waldemar Gurian") founded *[The Review of Politics](/wiki/The_Review_of_Politics "The Review of Politics")*, which quickly emerged as part of an international Catholic intellectual revival, offering an alternative vision to positivist philosophy.[[222]](#cite_note-223) The College of Arts and Letters is distinguished for its contributions in the field of theology and religious studies,[[223]](#cite_note-224) while its affiliated Medieval Institute is the largest center for [medieval studies](/wiki/Medieval_studies "Medieval studies") in North America.[[224]](#cite_note-225)
The university has many multi-disciplinary research institutes, including the Medieval Institute, the Kellogg Institute for International Studies, the Kroc Institute for International [Peace Studies](/wiki/Peace_and_conflict_studies "Peace and conflict studies"), and the Center for Social Concerns.[[225]](#cite_note-226) Recent research includes work on family conflict and child development,[[226]](#cite_note-227)[[227]](#cite_note-228) [genome mapping](/wiki/Genome_project "Genome project"),[[228]](#cite_note-229) the increasing [trade deficit](/wiki/Balance_of_trade "Balance of trade") of the United States with China,[[229]](#cite_note-230) studies in [fluid mechanics](/wiki/Fluid_mechanics "Fluid mechanics"),[[230]](#cite_note-231) [computational science and engineering](/wiki/Computational_science_and_engineering "Computational science and engineering"),[[231]](#cite_note-232) [supramolecular chemistry](/wiki/Supramolecular_chemistry "Supramolecular chemistry"),[[232]](#cite_note-233) and marketing trends on the Internet.[[233]](#cite_note-234) As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit), the university was home to the [Notre Dame Global Adaptation Index](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Global_Adaptation_Index "Notre Dame Global Adaptation Index"), which ranks countries annually based on how vulnerable they are to climate change and how prepared they are to adapt.[[234]](#cite_note-235)

In the fiscal 2019, the university received the all-time high research funding of $180.6 million, an increase of $100 million from 2009 and a 27 percent increase from the previous year, with funded projects including vector-borne diseases, urbanism, environmental design, cancer, psychology, economics, philosophy of religion, particle physics, nanotechnology, and hypersonics.[[235]](#cite_note-236) Notre Dame has a strong background in the humanities, with 65 [National Endowment for the Humanities](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Humanities "National Endowment for the Humanities") fellowships, more than any other university. Focus areas include anti-poverty economic strategy, the premier Medieval Institute, Latino studies, sacred music, Italian studies, Catholic studies, psychology, aging and stress, social good, and theology.[[236]](#cite_note-237) In the sciences, research focuses and specialized centers include the Harper Cancer Research Institute, the Boler-Parseghian Center for Rare and Neglected Diseases, the Center for Nano Science and Technology, the Center for Stem Cells and Regenerative Medicine, the Eck Institute for Global Health, the Joint Institute for Nuclear Astrophysics, the University of Notre Dame Environmental Research Center, Topology and Quantum Field Theory, the Nuclear Physics Research Group, and the Environmental Change Initiative.[[237]](#cite_note-238)
