As of Fall 2024, the Notre Dame student body consisted of 8,880 [undergraduates](/wiki/Undergraduate_education "Undergraduate education") and 4,162 [graduate](/wiki/Graduate_school "Graduate school") and professional (Law, M.Div., Business, MEd) students.[[239]](#cite_note-243) An estimated 21–24 percent of students are children of [alumni](/wiki/Alumnus "Alumnus"),[[240]](#cite_note-244) and the student body represents all 50 states and 88 countries. Thirty-seven percent of students come from the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), 40 percent of students are U.S. students of color,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and eight percent are international citizens.[[191]](#cite_note-admissions.nd.edu-192)

### Residence halls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Residence halls")]

Main article: [University of Notre Dame residence halls](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame_residence_halls "University of Notre Dame residence halls")

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Howard_Hall.jpg/250px-Howard_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Howard_Hall.jpg)

Howard Hall, one of 15 women's dormitories

The residence halls, or dorms, are the focus of student social and intramural life.[[241]](#cite_note-245)[[242]](#cite_note-:0-246)[[243]](#cite_note-247) Each hall is led by a rector, a full-time, live-in professional who serves as leader, chief administrator, community builder and university resource to the residents, and is a priest, religious sister or brother, or a layperson trained in ministry or education.[[244]](#cite_note-248) Rectors direct the hall community, foster bonding, and often coordinate with professors, academic advisors, and counselors to watch over students and assist them with their personal development.[[245]](#cite_note-249) Rectors select, hire, train, and supervise hall staff: [resident assistants](/wiki/Resident_assistant "Resident assistant") (required to be seniors) and assistant rectors (graduate students).[[246]](#cite_note-250) Many residence halls also have a priest or faculty members in residence as faculty fellows, who provide an additional academic and intellectual experience to residential hall life.[[247]](#cite_note-251) Every hall has its own chapel, dedicated to the hall's patron saint, and liturgical schedule with masses celebrated multiple times a week during the academic year, in the tradition of individual chapels at English [university colleges](/wiki/Colleges_within_universities_in_the_United_Kingdom "Colleges within universities in the United Kingdom").[[248]](#cite_note-252)

[Fraternities and sororities](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities") are not allowed on campus, as they are described as in opposition to the university's educational and residential mission.[[249]](#cite_note-253) The residential halls provide the social and communal aspect of fraternities, but in line with the university's policy of inclusion and zero tolerance of hazing.[[250]](#cite_note-254)[[251]](#cite_note-255)

Over four-fifths of students live in the same residence hall for three consecutive years and about one-third of students live in the same residence hall for all four years As of October 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit).[[252]](#cite_note-256) A new policy was put into effect beginning in 2018, which required undergraduates to live on-campus for three years. In spring 2019, the university also announced a policy (that has since been reversed) that prohibited students living off campus from participating in dorm activities, such as intramural sports and dorm dances.[[253]](#cite_note-257) Most intramural (interhall) sports are based on residence hall teams, where the university offers the only non-military academy program of full-contact intramural American football.[[254]](#cite_note-Life_FAQ-258) At the end of the interhall football season, the championship game is played in [Notre Dame Stadium](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Stadium "Notre Dame Stadium").[[255]](#cite_note-Dillon-Hall-Football-2017-259)

### Student clubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Student clubs")]

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Knight_of_Columbus_Notre_Dame.jpg/250px-Knight_of_Columbus_Notre_Dame.jpg)](/wiki/File:Knight_of_Columbus_Notre_Dame.jpg)

The hall of the Notre Dame Council of the Knights of Columbus

There are over 400 active student clubs at the University of Notre Dame, with the financial oversight of each club delegated by the student-run [Club Coordination Council](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Club_Coordination_Council "Notre Dame Club Coordination Council").[[256]](#cite_note-260) The university subsidizes clubs, providing almost 15 percent of clubs' collective projected expenditures of $2.2 million during the 2018–2019 academic year.[[257]](#cite_note-261) There are a variety of student clubs on campus, including nine for students from different states,[[258]](#cite_note-262) about three dozen clubs that represent different nationalities and origins,[[259]](#cite_note-263) and clubs dedicated to Catholic theology,[[260]](#cite_note-264) diverse faith practices, social service, political advocacy and awareness, competitive athletics, professional development and networking, performing arts, academic debate, foreign affairs, fraternal brotherhood, women's empowerment, and many other interests.[[261]](#cite_note-265) The university hosts their annual Student Activities Fair early in the fall semester for all students interested in joining clubs or other student organizations.[[262]](#cite_note-266)

### Student union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Student union")]

The Notre Dame Student Union is divided into nine branches, as articulated in Article I, Section 2 of the *Constitution of the Undergraduate Student Body of the University of Notre Dame du Lac*.[[263]](#cite_note-267)

#### Senate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Senate")]

The Student Senate is the legislative body of the Student Union that shall formulate and advance the position of the undergraduate student body on all issues concerning campus life. The Senate is composed of representatives from each of the university's undergraduate residence halls, and other representatives from the Student Union.[[264]](#cite_note-268)

#### Executive Cabinet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Executive Cabinet")]

The Executive Cabinet is led by the Student Body President and Vice President. Membership is composed of Cabinet Directors who lead various departments.[[265]](#cite_note-269) The Executive Cabinet is tasked with advancing the agenda of the President and Vice-President through working to protect the interests of students in all areas of university life, provide services to the undergraduate student body, and advance the policy priorities of the broader Student Union.[[266]](#cite_note-270)

#### Student Union Board

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Student Union Board")]

According to the Student Union Board's (SUB) mission statement, "The SUB Programming Body shall enhance undergraduate student life by providing undergraduate student services and social, intellectual, and cultural opportunities that respond to the needs and wants of the undergraduate student body in the most efficient manner possible."[[267]](#cite_note-271) SUB is divided into seven committees, which consist of Acousticafé, AnTostal, Community Engagement, Concerts, First Look Into Programming (FLIP), Live Entertainment, and Signature Events.[[268]](#cite_note-272)

#### Hall Presidents Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Hall Presidents Council")]

The Hall Presidents Council (HPC) is responsible for planning, funding, and executing residence hall events. The council is composed of leadership from all of Notre Dame's undergraduate residence halls, in addition to council leaders.[[269]](#cite_note-273)

#### Club Coordination Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Club Coordination Council")]

The Club Coordination Council (CCC) is responsible for communicating issues facing undergraduate club issues, providing funding for undergraduate clubs, serving as the representative body of undergraduate student clubs, and working with student clubs to ensure that clubs can coordinate their programming of activities.[[270]](#cite_note-constitution-274) The CCC oversees around 400 student clubs,[[271]](#cite_note-Student_Groups-275) each of which serves a unique purpose.[[272]](#cite_note-276)[[273]](#cite_note-fox-news-scop-denial-277) The approval of the council, along with that of the Notre Dame Student Activities Office, is a requirement for official recognition of student clubs.[[274]](#cite_note-scop-denial-ncregister-278)[[275]](#cite_note-irish-rover-scop-279)[[276]](#cite_note-ClubRespect-280)

#### Class Councils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Class Councils")]

There are four Class Councils, one representing each undergraduate class level: First-Year, Sophomore, Junior, and Senior Class Councils. They each promote the wellbeing of their respective classes by sponsoring functions that promote unity among class members. Each class is represented by four elected class officers, including a President, Vice-President, Treasurer, and Secretary. In addition to the officers, the Class Councils are made up of members from all across campus that work together to plan events.[[277]](#cite_note-281)

#### Off-Campus Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Off-Campus Council")]

The Off-Campus Council represents any undergraduate students not residing on the campus of the university. They provide to the needs of those students, given that the issues differ from those residing in the dorms.[[278]](#cite_note-282)

#### Financial Management Board

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Financial Management Board")]

The Financial Management Board (FMB) is responsible for the creation and maintenance of a budget for the Student Union. FMB conducts an annual hearing to ensure that undergraduate funds are allocated fairly, known as the Annual Allocation Hearing. The FMB representative from each organization prepares a budget and then meets with the Student Union Treasurer to discuss their organization's monetary needs, which are presented at the Annual Allocation Hearing.[[279]](#cite_note-283) After the allocations are agreed upon by its members, the budget is proposed to the Senate Committee on the Budget, which either approves or rejects these allocations.[[280]](#cite_note-284)

#### Judicial Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Judicial Council")]

The Judicial Council is tasked with overseeing the ethical behavior of Student Union leaders, maintaining the constitutional conduct of the Student Union, administering its elections, and providing support through Peer Advocates to students navigating the Office of Community Standards' hearings and conferences.[[281]](#cite_note-285)

### Student events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Student events")]

Website BestColleges.com ranks the university's [intramural sports](/wiki/Intramural_sports "Intramural sports") program as number one in the country in 2021.[[282]](#cite_note-286) Over 700 teams participate each year in the annual [Bookstore Basketball](/wiki/Bookstore_Basketball "Bookstore Basketball") tournament;[[283]](#cite_note-287) while the Notre Dame Men's [Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") Club hosts the annual [Bengal Bouts](/wiki/Bengal_Bouts "Bengal Bouts") tournament to raise money for the [Holy Cross Missions](/wiki/Congregation_of_Holy_Cross#The_Mission_of_Holy_Cross_in_Bangladesh "Congregation of Holy Cross") in Bangladesh.[[284]](#cite_note-288) In the fall, the Notre Dame Women's Boxing Club hosts an annual Baraka Bouts tournament that raises money for the Congregation of the Holy Cross Missions in Uganda.[[285]](#cite_note-289)

Many of the most popular student events held on campus are organized by the 33 residential halls. Among these, the most notable are the [Keenan Revue](/wiki/Keenan_Revue "Keenan Revue"), the [Fisher Hall](/wiki/Fisher_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Fisher Hall (University of Notre Dame)") Regatta, [Howard Hall](/wiki/Howard_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Howard Hall (University of Notre Dame)") Totter for Water, [Keenan Hall](/wiki/Keenan_Hall "Keenan Hall") Muddy Sunday, the [Morrissey Hall](/wiki/Morrissey_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Morrissey Hall (University of Notre Dame)") Medallion Hunt, the [Dillon Hall](/wiki/Dillon_Hall "Dillon Hall") Pep Rally, the [Keough Hall](/wiki/Keough_Hall "Keough Hall") Chariot Race and many others. Each dorm also hosts many formal and informal balls and dances each year.[[286]](#cite_note-290)

### Religious life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Religious life")]

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart_at_Night.jpg/250px-Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart_at_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart_at_Night.jpg)

Basilica of the Sacred Heart at night

While having a religious affiliation is not a criterion for admission, over 93 percent of students identify as Christian, with over 80 percent of those being Catholic.[[287]](#cite_note-291) There are 57 chapels on campus, including one in every residence hall. Collectively, [Catholic Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") is celebrated over 100 times per week on campus, and a large campus ministry program provides for the faith needs of the community.[[254]](#cite_note-Life_FAQ-258)[[288]](#cite_note-292)[[289]](#cite_note-293) There is also an active council of the [Knights of Columbus](/wiki/Knights_of_Columbus "Knights of Columbus") on campus, which is the oldest and largest college council of the international Catholic men's organization.[[290]](#cite_note-294)[[291]](#cite_note-295) Non-Catholic religious organizations on campus include the [Baptist Collegiate Ministry (BCM)](/wiki/Baptist_Student_Union "Baptist Student Union"), Jewish Club of Notre Dame, the Muslim Student Association, the Orthodox Christian Fellowship, the Chi Alpha Christian Fellowship, and many more.[[271]](#cite_note-Student_Groups-275)

The university is the major seat of the Congregation of Holy Cross (albeit not its official headquarters, which are in Rome).[[292]](#cite_note-296) Its main seminary, Moreau Seminary, is on the campus across St. Joseph Lake from the Main Building.[[293]](#cite_note-297) [Old College](/wiki/Old_College,_University_of_Notre_Dame "Old College, University of Notre Dame"), the oldest building on campus near the shore of St. Mary's Lake, houses undergraduate seminarians. Retired priests and brothers reside in Fatima House (a former retreat center), Holy Cross House, and Columba Hall near the Grotto.[[294]](#cite_note-298)

### Student-run media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Student-run media")]

Notre Dame students run nine media outlets: three newspapers, a radio and television station, and several magazines and journals.

The *[Scholastic](/wiki/Scholastic_(Notre_Dame_publication) "Scholastic (Notre Dame publication)")* magazine, begun as a one-page journal in 1876,[[295]](#cite_note-299) is issued twice monthly and claims to be the oldest continuous collegiate publication in the United States. The other magazine, *The Juggler*, is released twice a year and focuses on student literature and artwork.[[296]](#cite_note-Publications-300) The *Dome* yearbook is published annually. The newspapers have varying publication interests, with *[The Observer](/wiki/The_Observer_(Notre_Dame) "The Observer (Notre Dame)")* published daily and mainly reporting university and other news,[[297]](#cite_note-301) staffed by students from both Notre Dame and Saint Mary's College. Unlike *Scholastic* and *The Dome*, *The Observer* is an independent publication and does not have a faculty advisor or any editorial oversight from the university.[[298]](#cite_note-302) In 2003, when other students believed that the paper had a liberal bias, they started [*The* *Irish Rover*](/wiki/The_Irish_Rover_(newspaper) "The Irish Rover (newspaper)"), a print and digital newspaper published twice per month that features regular columns from alumni and faculty and coverage of campus matters. As of 2005, *The Observer* and the *Irish Rover* were distributed to all students.[[296]](#cite_note-Publications-300) In Spring 2008, *Beyond Politics*, an undergraduate journal for political science research, made its debut.[[299]](#cite_note-303) In May 2023, a professor at the university, Tamara Kay, sued the Rover for defamation. The case was dismissed, and Kay filed an appeal in February 2024.[[300]](#cite_note-304)

[WSND-FM](/wiki/WSND-FM "WSND-FM") serves the student body and the larger South Bend community at 88.9 FM, offering students a chance to become involved in bringing classical music, fine arts and educational programming, and alternative rock to the airwaves. Another radio station, WVFI, began as a partner of WSND-FM; it now airs independently and is streamed on the Internet.[[301]](#cite_note-305)

The television station (NDtv) grew from one show in 2002 to a full 24-hour channel with original programming by 2006.[[302]](#cite_note-306)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Notre Dame Fighting Irish](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish "Notre Dame Fighting Irish")

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Notre-dame-stadium.jpg/250px-Notre-dame-stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Notre-dame-stadium.jpg)

[Notre Dame Stadium](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Stadium "Notre Dame Stadium")

Notre Dame's sports teams are known as the Fighting Irish. They compete as a member of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) [Division I](/wiki/Division_I_(NCAA) "Division I (NCAA)"), primarily competing in the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") (ACC) for all sports—except football and hockey —since the 2013–14 school year. Men's ice hockey is played in the [Big Ten conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_conference "Big Ten conference").[[303]](#cite_note-Athletics-307) Notre Dame men compete in baseball, basketball, cross country, fencing, football, golf, ice hockey, lacrosse, soccer, swimming and diving, tennis, track and field, and volleyball. Women's sports are basketball, cross country, fencing, golf, lacrosse, rowing, soccer, softball, swimming and diving, tennis, track and field, and volleyball. The football team competes as a [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/Football_Bowl_Subdivision "Football Bowl Subdivision") (FBS) [Independent](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_FBS_independent_schools "NCAA Division I FBS independent schools")[[304]](#cite_note-308) since its inception in 1887, except for 2020, when it competed as part of the Atlantic Coast Conference. Both fencing teams compete in the [Midwest Fencing Conference](/wiki/Midwest_Fencing_Conference "Midwest Fencing Conference").[[305]](#cite_note-309)

[![Large crowds seated in the Football Satdium](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Fans_and_players_gather_for_a_football_game_Sept._6%2C_2014%2C_at_Notre_Dame_Stadium_in_South_Bend%2C_Ind_140906-D-KC128-220.jpg/250px-Fans_and_players_gather_for_a_football_game_Sept._6%2C_2014%2C_at_Notre_Dame_Stadium_in_South_Bend%2C_Ind_140906-D-KC128-220.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fans_and_players_gather_for_a_football_game_Sept._6,_2014,_at_Notre_Dame_Stadium_in_South_Bend,_Ind_140906-D-KC128-220.jpg)

Football stadium during a game

Notre Dame's sports conference affiliations, except football and fencing, changed in July 2013 because of [major conference realignment](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9313_NCAA_conference_realignment "2010–13 NCAA conference realignment"), and its fencing affiliation changed in July 2014. The Irish left the Big East for the ACC during a [prolonged period of instability](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9313_Big_East_Conference_realignment "2010–13 Big East Conference realignment") in the Big East;[[306]](#cite_note-310)[[307]](#cite_note-311) while they maintain their football independence, they have committed to playing five games per season against ACC opponents.[[308]](#cite_note-312) After Notre Dame joined the ACC, the conference announced it would add fencing as a sponsored sport beginning in the 2014–15 school year.[[309]](#cite_note-313)

There are many theories behind the adoption of the team name[[310]](#cite_note-314) but the Fighting Irish name was used in the early 1920s with respect to the football team, and alumnus [Francis Wallace](/wiki/Francis_Wallace_(writer) "Francis Wallace (writer)") popularized it in his *[New York Daily News](/wiki/Daily_News_(New_York) "Daily News (New York)")* columns.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESperber200276-315) Notre Dame's official colors are gold and navy blue.[[312]](#cite_note-Gold_And_Blue-316) Green is sometimes worn because of the Fighting Irish nickname.[[312]](#cite_note-Gold_And_Blue-316)

The [Notre Dame Leprechaun](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Leprechaun "Notre Dame Leprechaun") is the mascot of the athletic teams. Created by [Theodore W. Drake](/wiki/Theodore_W._Drake "Theodore W. Drake") in 1964, the leprechaun was first used on the football [pocket schedule](/wiki/Pocket_schedule "Pocket schedule") and later the football program covers. [*Time*](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") featured it on a November 1964 cover.[[313]](#cite_note-317) Since its inception in 2011, [Fighting Irish Media](/wiki/Fighting_Irish_Media "Fighting Irish Media") (FIM), made up of part-time student workers and full time producers, has filmed nearly all Fighting Irish sporting events for live digital and linear broadcasts.[[314]](#cite_note-318) With the installation of a videoboard in Notre Dame Stadium in Fall 2017, FIM has taken over video board production for all Fighting Irish teams.[[315]](#cite_note-319) In 2014, the University of Notre Dame and [Under Armour](/wiki/Under_Armour "Under Armour") reached an agreement whereby the company provides uniforms, apparel, equipment, and monetary compensation to Notre Dame for 10 years. This contract, worth almost $100 million, was the most lucrative in the history of the NCAA at that time.[[316]](#cite_note-320) According to some analysts without direct connection to the university or its athletic department, Notre Dame promotes [Muscular Christianity](/wiki/Muscular_Christianity "Muscular Christianity") through its athletic programs.[[317]](#cite_note-C&P-321)[[318]](#cite_note-Sponsors-322)

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Football")]

Main article: [Notre Dame Fighting Irish football](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/2004_Notre_Dame-Navy_Game.jpg/250px-2004_Notre_Dame-Navy_Game.jpg)](/wiki/File:2004_Notre_Dame-Navy_Game.jpg)

Notre Dame playing against Navy

The Notre Dame football team's history began when the [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football") team brought the game to Notre Dame in 1887 and played against a group of students.[[319]](#cite_note-323) Since then, 13 Fighting Irish teams have won consensus [national championships](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS") (although the university only claims 11),[[303]](#cite_note-Athletics-307) along with another nine teams being named national champions by at least one source.[[320]](#cite_note-324) The program has the most members in the [College Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/College_Football_Hall_of_Fame "College Football Hall of Fame"),[[321]](#cite_note-325) is tied with [Ohio State](/wiki/Ohio_State_Buckeyes_football "Ohio State Buckeyes football") and [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma_Sooners_football "Oklahoma Sooners football") for the most [Heisman Trophies](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") won by players,[[322]](#cite_note-326) and has the 3rd highest [winning percentage](/wiki/Winning_percentage "Winning percentage") in NCAA history, behind [Ohio State](/wiki/Ohio_State_Buckeyes_football "Ohio State Buckeyes football") and [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama_Crimson_Tide_football "Alabama Crimson Tide football").[[323]](#cite_note-327) Notre Dame has accumulated many [rivals](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football_rivalries "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football rivalries"); the annual game against [USC](/wiki/USC_Trojans_football "USC Trojans football") for the [Jeweled Shillelagh](/wiki/Jeweled_Shillelagh "Jeweled Shillelagh") has been described as one of the greatest in college football.[[324]](#cite_note-greatest-328)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Knute_Rockne_on_ship%27s_deck.jpg/250px-Knute_Rockne_on_ship%27s_deck.jpg)](/wiki/File:Knute_Rockne_on_ship%27s_deck.jpg)

Coach [Knute Rockne](/wiki/Knute_Rockne "Knute Rockne")

George Gipp was the school's legendary football player of the late 1910s.[[325]](#cite_note-329) In 1928, coach Knute Rockne used his final conversation with the dying Gipp to inspire the Notre Dame team to beat [Army](/wiki/Army_Black_Knights_football "Army Black Knights football") and "win one for the Gipper"; that scene became the climax of the 1940 film *[Knute Rockne, All American](/wiki/Knute_Rockne,_All_American "Knute Rockne, All American")*, starring [Pat O'Brien](/wiki/Pat_O%27Brien_(actor) "Pat O'Brien (actor)") as Rockne and [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") as Gipp.[[326]](#cite_note-330)

The team competes in the 80,795-seat Notre Dame Stadium.[[327]](#cite_note-331) The current head coach is [Marcus Freeman](/wiki/Marcus_Freeman "Marcus Freeman"), who was promoted to head coach after [Brian Kelly](/wiki/Brian_Kelly_(American_football_coach) "Brian Kelly (American football coach)") departed Notre Dame to coach at [LSU](/wiki/LSU_Tigers_football_team "LSU Tigers football team") at the end of the [2021 regular season](/wiki/2021_Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football_team "2021 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team"). [Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes") ranked the program college football's eighth most valuable for its average annual revenue of $120 million. It has a TV contract with NBC worth an estimated $15 million per year and one of the country's largest fan bases.[[328]](#cite_note-332)

#### Football game-day traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Football game-day traditions")]

During home games, activities occur all over campus and dorms decorate their halls with a traditional item (e.g., [Zahm Hall](/wiki/Zahm_Hall_(University_of_Notre_Dame) "Zahm Hall (University of Notre Dame)")'s two-story banner). Traditional activities begin at midnight with the Drummers' Circle, involving the [Band of the Fighting Irish](/wiki/Band_of_the_Fighting_Irish "Band of the Fighting Irish")'s drumline beginning the other festivities that will continue the rest of the game day. Later that day, the trumpet section will play the [Notre Dame Victory March](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Victory_March "Notre Dame Victory March") and the Notre Dame Alma Mater under the dome. The entire band will play a concert at the steps of Bond Hall, then march into the stadium, leading fans and students alike across campus to the game.[[329]](#cite_note-ND_Gamedayl-333) At the end of each game, the Notre Dame Marching Band plays the alma mater.

### Men's basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Men's basketball")]

Main article: [Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's basketball](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_men%27s_basketball "Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's basketball")

[![See caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Purcell_Pavillion_ND_interior.jpg/330px-Purcell_Pavillion_ND_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Purcell_Pavillion_ND_interior.jpg)

The Joyce Center, home stadium for Notre Dame's basketball teams

As of the 2020–2021 season, the men's basketball team has over 1,910 wins and appeared in 36 [NCAA tournaments](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship")[[330]](#cite_note-334)[[331]](#cite_note-BBall-335) Former player [Austin Carr](/wiki/Austin_Carr "Austin Carr") holds the record for most points scored in a single game of the tournament with 61.[[332]](#cite_note-336) Although the team has never won the NCAA Tournament, they were named by the [Helms Athletic Foundation](/wiki/Helms_Athletic_Foundation "Helms Athletic Foundation") as national champions twice.[[331]](#cite_note-BBall-335) The team has orchestrated a number of [upsets](/wiki/Upset_(competition) "Upset (competition)") of top-ranked teams, the most notable of which was ending [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA_Bruins_men%27s_basketball "UCLA Bruins men's basketball")'s record 88-game winning streak in 1974.[[333]](#cite_note-337) Notre Dame has beaten an additional eight number-one teams, and those nine wins rank second, to UCLA's 10, all-time in wins against the top team.[[331]](#cite_note-BBall-335)

### Other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Other sports")]

See also: [Notre Dame Fighting Irish women's basketball](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_women%27s_basketball "Notre Dame Fighting Irish women's basketball"), [Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's ice hockey](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_men%27s_ice_hockey "Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's ice hockey"), and [Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's lacrosse](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_men%27s_lacrosse "Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's lacrosse")

Notre Dame has won an additional 15 national championships in sports other than football. Four teams have won multiple national championships; the fencing team leads with 10,[[334]](#cite_note-338) followed by the men's lacrosse,[[335]](#cite_note-339) men's tennis, and women's soccer teams with two each.[[336]](#cite_note-340)[[337]](#cite_note-past-341) The [men's cross country](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_men%27s_cross_country "Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's cross country")[[337]](#cite_note-past-341) and golf[[337]](#cite_note-past-341) teams have won one and Notre Dame women's basketball has won two.[[338]](#cite_note-342) In the first 10 years that Notre Dame competed in the Big East Conference its teams won a total of 64 championships.[[339]](#cite_note-343) As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit), the women's swimming and diving team holds the Big East record for consecutive conference championships in any sport with 14 straight conference titles (1997–2010).[[340]](#cite_note-344)

### Band and "Victory March"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Band and \"Victory March\"")]

The Band of the Fighting Irish was formed in 1846 and is the oldest university band in continuous existence.[[341]](#cite_note-345) The [marching band](/wiki/Marching_band "Marching band") plays at home games for most sports. It regularly plays the school's [fight song](/wiki/Fight_song "Fight song"), the Notre Dame "[Victory March](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Victory_March "Notre Dame Victory March")", identified as the most played and most famous fight song by [Northern Illinois](/wiki/Northern_Illinois_University "Northern Illinois University") professor [William Studwell](/wiki/William_Studwell "William Studwell").[[342]](#cite_note-leroux-346) According to *College Fight Songs: An Annotated Anthology* published in 1998, the "Victory March" is the greatest fight song.[[342]](#cite_note-leroux-346) It was honored by the [National Music Council](/wiki/National_Music_Council "National Music Council") as a "Landmark of American Music" during the [United States Bicentennial](/wiki/United_States_Bicentennial "United States Bicentennial").[[343]](#cite_note-347) The song is featured in the films *[Knute Rockne, All American](/wiki/Knute_Rockne,_All_American "Knute Rockne, All American")*, *[Airplane!](/wiki/Airplane! "Airplane!")* and *[Rudy](/wiki/Rudy_(film) "Rudy (film)")*.[[344]](#cite_note-348)

## Alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Alumni")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Notre Dame alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Notre_Dame_alumni "List of University of Notre Dame alumni") and [List of University of Notre Dame athletes](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Notre_Dame_athletes "List of University of Notre Dame athletes").

The school has over 130,000 alumni and 275 alumni clubs around the world.[[345]](#cite_note-349)[[346]](#cite_note-350) Notre Dame is ranked among the universities with strongest alumni networks.[[347]](#cite_note-:5-351) Many give the university yearly monetary support. Notre Dame is ranked among schools with the highest alumni donation rates.[[348]](#cite_note-352) A school-record of 53.2 percent of alumni donating was set in 2006.[[349]](#cite_note-353) Many buildings, including residence halls, on campus are named for major donors.[[350]](#cite_note-354)[[351]](#cite_note-355) Classroom buildings,[[352]](#cite_note-356) and the performing arts center are also named for donors.[[97]](#cite_note-DPAC-98)

Alumni working in politics include [state governors](/wiki/Governor_(United_States) "Governor (United States)"),[[353]](#cite_note-357) members of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"),[[354]](#cite_note-358) and former [United States Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") [Condoleezza Rice](/wiki/Condoleezza_Rice "Condoleezza Rice").[[355]](#cite_note-359) Notable alumni from the College of Science are [Eric F. Wieschaus](/wiki/Eric_F._Wieschaus "Eric F. Wieschaus"), winner of the 1995 Nobel Prize in medicine,[[356]](#cite_note-360) and [Philip Majerus](/wiki/Philip_Majerus "Philip Majerus"), discoverer of the cardioprotective effects of aspirin.[[357]](#cite_note-361) Many university officials are alumni, including the current president, John Jenkins and incoming President Robert Dowd, a professor of Political Science who specializes in African Studies.[[358]](#cite_note-362)[[359]](#cite_note-363) Alumni in media include talk show hosts [Regis Philbin](/wiki/Regis_Philbin "Regis Philbin")[[360]](#cite_note-364) and [Phil Donahue](/wiki/Phil_Donahue "Phil Donahue"),[[361]](#cite_note-365) and television and radio personalities such as [Mike Golic](/wiki/Mike_Golic "Mike Golic")[[362]](#cite_note-366) and [Hannah Storm](/wiki/Hannah_Storm "Hannah Storm").[[363]](#cite_note-367) A number of sports alumni have continued their careers in professional sports, such as [Joe Theismann](/wiki/Joe_Theismann "Joe Theismann"), [Joe Montana](/wiki/Joe_Montana "Joe Montana"),[[364]](#cite_note-368) [Tim Brown](/wiki/Tim_Brown_(American_football) "Tim Brown (American football)"), [Ross Browner](/wiki/Ross_Browner "Ross Browner"), [Rocket Ismail](/wiki/Rocket_Ismail "Rocket Ismail"), [Ruth Riley](/wiki/Ruth_Riley "Ruth Riley"), [Jeff Samardzija](/wiki/Jeff_Samardzija "Jeff Samardzija"),[[365]](#cite_note-369) [Jerome Bettis](/wiki/Jerome_Bettis "Jerome Bettis"), [Justin Tuck](/wiki/Justin_Tuck "Justin Tuck"), [Craig Counsell](/wiki/Craig_Counsell "Craig Counsell"), [Skylar Diggins-Smith](/wiki/Skylar_Diggins-Smith "Skylar Diggins-Smith"), [Brett Lebda](/wiki/Brett_Lebda "Brett Lebda"), Olympic fencing gold medalist [Mariel Zagunis](/wiki/Mariel_Zagunis "Mariel Zagunis") and two-time bronze medalist [Nick Itkin](/wiki/Nick_Itkin "Nick Itkin"), professional boxer [Mike Lee](/wiki/Mike_Lee_(boxer) "Mike Lee (boxer)"), former football coaches such as [Charlie Weis](/wiki/Charlie_Weis "Charlie Weis"),[[366]](#cite_note-Weis-370) [Frank Leahy](/wiki/Frank_Leahy "Frank Leahy") and [Knute Rockne](/wiki/Knute_Rockne "Knute Rockne"),[[367]](#cite_note-371) and [Basketball Hall of Famers](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") [Austin Carr](/wiki/Austin_Carr "Austin Carr") and [Adrian Dantley](/wiki/Adrian_Dantley "Adrian Dantley"). Other notable alumni include prominent businessman [Edward J. DeBartolo Jr.](/wiki/Edward_J._DeBartolo_Jr. "Edward J. DeBartolo Jr.") and [astronaut](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut") [Jim Wetherbee](/wiki/Jim_Wetherbee "Jim Wetherbee").[[368]](#cite_note-372)[[369]](#cite_note-373) Two alumni have received the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") ([Alan Page](/wiki/Alan_Page "Alan Page") and [Edward J. DeBartolo Jr.](/wiki/Edward_J._DeBartolo_Jr. "Edward J. DeBartolo Jr.")), and two the [Congressional Gold Medal](/wiki/Congressional_Gold_Medal "Congressional Gold Medal") ([Thomas Anthony Dooley III](/wiki/Thomas_Anthony_Dooley_III "Thomas Anthony Dooley III") and [Bill Hanzlik](/wiki/Bill_Hanzlik "Bill Hanzlik")).

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The University of Notre Dame is the setting of several works of fiction, as well as the alma mater of some fictional characters.[[370]](#cite_note-:9-374) In mid-20th century America, it became "perhaps the most popular symbol of Catholicism" as noted by *The Routledge Companion to Religion and Popular Culture*:

> By combining religion, ethnicity, masculinity, and athletics into a potent mixture of an aggressive and uniquely Catholic gospel of athletics, Notre Dame football became the emblematic program that represented American Catholic self-identity.[[371]](#cite_note-375)

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Film")]

* *[Knute Rockne, All American](/wiki/Knute_Rockne,_All_American "Knute Rockne, All American")* is a 1940 biographical film which tells the story of Knute Rockne, Notre Dame football coach.[[372]](#cite_note-376)
* The "Win one for the Gipper" speech was parodied in the 1980 film *[Airplane!](/wiki/Airplane! "Airplane!")* when, with the "Victory March" rising to a crescendo in the background, Dr. Rumak (played by [Leslie Nielsen](/wiki/Leslie_Nielsen "Leslie Nielsen")) urged reluctant pilot Ted Striker (played by [Robert Hays](/wiki/Robert_Hays "Robert Hays")) to "win just one for the Zipper", Striker's war buddy George Zipp. The "Victory March" also plays during the film's credits.[[373]](#cite_note-377)[[374]](#cite_note-378)
* [*Rudy*](/wiki/Rudy_(film) "Rudy (film)") is a 1993 account of the life of [Daniel "Rudy" Ruettiger](/wiki/Rudy_Ruettiger "Rudy Ruettiger"), who harbored dreams of playing football at Notre Dame despite significant obstacles.[[375]](#cite_note-379)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Television")]

* President [Josiah Bartlet](/wiki/Josiah_Bartlet "Josiah Bartlet") from the show *[The West Wing](/wiki/The_West_Wing "The West Wing")* is a Notre Dame graduate and First Lady [Abigail Bartlet](/wiki/Abigail_Bartlet "Abigail Bartlet") attended [Saint Mary's College](/wiki/Saint_Mary%27s_College_(Indiana) "Saint Mary's College (Indiana)"). [Danny Concannon](/wiki/Danny_Concannon "Danny Concannon"), a member of the White House press corps, is also a graduate of Notre Dame. Actor [Martin Sheen](/wiki/Martin_Sheen "Martin Sheen") specifically asked that his character be a Notre Dame alumnus due to the Catholicism shared by both the actor and the character.[[376]](#cite_note-380)[[370]](#cite_note-:9-374)[[377]](#cite_note-381)

### Other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Other media")]

* One of the music videos for the song "[This Too Shall Pass](/wiki/This_Too_Shall_Pass_(OK_Go_song) "This Too Shall Pass (OK Go song)")" by [OK Go](/wiki/OK_Go "OK Go") was created in collaboration with the [University of Notre Dame Marching Band](/wiki/Band_of_the_Fighting_Irish "Band of the Fighting Irish") and was shot on the university campus.[[378]](#cite_note-382)[[379]](#cite_note-383)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Notre_Dame&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** The university's campus actually contains two lakes, but according to legend, when Sorin arrived at the site everything was frozen, so he thought there was only one lake and named the university accordingly.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-240)** Other consists of [Multiracial Americans](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans") & those who prefer to not say.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-241)** The percentage of students who received an income-based federal [Pell grant](/wiki/Pell_grant "Pell grant") intended for low-income students.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-242)** The percentage of students who are a part of the [American middle class](/wiki/American_middle_class "American middle class") at the bare minimum.
